narration: Mapping the seafloor by hand is slow and inconsistent. With the Israel Oceanographic and Limnological Research institute, we built a pipeline that turns multibeam sonar surveys from the research vessel Bat-Galim into per-pixel maps of three seabed types: rock, shallow rock, and sand. Automatically, and reproducibly.

Every survey gives us three physical layers on a one-metre grid. Bathymetry is depth. Backscatter measures how acoustically hard the seafloor is. Slope is steepness. Marine geologists hand-draw the ground truth as polygons. But there's a catch. Across four surveys we have only one to two square kilometres of labelled seabed, about a hundred times less than the reference study. And backscatter is stored differently per survey, from raw decibels to grayscale images: a real domain shift the model has to survive.

The pipeline is deterministic and config-driven. Every layer is reprojected onto one shared grid in UTM zone 36 north. The expert polygons are rasterised into per-pixel labels. Then each survey is cut into overlapping tiles, 128 metres square at one metre per pixel, so every tile carries a three-band feature stack and a matching label.

Tiles come in three modes. The standard grid is axis-aligned. The rotated grid follows the annotations, not the compass. And an augmentation mode re-extracts that rotated grid at jittered angles and offsets, turning roughly 250 base tiles into over 900 for training.

Because the pixels are physical measurements, augmentation follows a strict contract. Only rigid geometry is allowed: rotations, flips, and genuine re-extraction. Brightness, noise, and zoom are forbidden; they would corrupt the physics. Splits are spatial only, never random, because overlapping tiles would leak between train and test. The loss ignores no-data pixels. And backscatter is normalised per survey, which bridges that domain shift.

We compare two model families on the same three bands. A compact U-Net, about 1.9 million parameters, learns spatial context. Against it, per-pixel tree baselines: a random forest and gradient boosting, which see one pixel at a time. Feature importance confirms the intuition: depth carries the strongest signal, then backscatter, then slope.

We evaluate two ways. The development split carves each survey into separate train, validation, and test bands with buffers, so no pixel is shared. The honest test is leave-one-polygon-out: train on three surveys, predict the fourth, and repeat. That measures whether the model generalises to a survey it has never seen.

The results are strong. The three-band U-Net reaches a macro-Dice of 0.78 within surveys, and 0.61 across unseen surveys, beating the two-band variant and the tree baselines on every metric. Rock is the standout: robust everywhere, and on the strongest survey its cross-survey Dice climbs to 0.98. Sand follows closely. The model maps the hard substrate that matters most for hazard and habitat work.

Seen as full maps, the story holds. Against the ground truth, the U-Net recovers the rock outcrops cleanly. The tree baselines capture the broad structure but leave salt-and-pepper noise, which an edge-aware spatial filter, guided by depth, smooths away for a small, consistent gain.

This is the pipeline running tile by tile. Each frame shows the input bands and every model's prediction, while the full-survey map fills in live. It's how we caught a labelling bug: two of the largest annotations were silently dropped, and the map made it obvious.

So where does this land? Rock classification is reliable enough to be useful for hazard and habitat mapping. The ceiling isn't the architecture; it's data. The highest-value next steps are more annotation, a hillshade fourth band, engineered multi-scale features, and ensembling. The infrastructure for all of it already exists.
From raw multibeam survey to a trained, honestly-evaluated seabed classifier, fully reproducible. Built for IOLR, as part of Code4Good at Reichman University.
